perturbation.original_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the initial six months of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the duration of the license granted in Article 3.00 to the 'initial six months of the Option Period' while later articles, such as 3.01, 3.02, 3.03, and 3.04 continue to refer to the license grant during the 'duration of the Option Period,' this creates an in-text contradiction. It's unclear whether those later articles apply only to the initial six months or to the entire Option Period, creating ambiguity and uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.01 Company further grants to Investor, during the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) to use the Technical Information to practice the methods described and claimed in the Patents and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using the methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the license duration in Article 3.00 to the 'initial six months' of the Option Period. However, Article 3.01 grants a license 'during the duration of the Option Period,' creating a direct contradiction. The duration of the license in 3.00 is now inconsistent with the duration specified in 3.01, causing uncertainty and potential unenforceability. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction,' where different sections provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.03 If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, the license grants set forth in Articles 3.00 and 3.01 will become exclusive to Investor for a perpetual term, shall not be subject to a licensing fee, the granted licenses in favor of the Investor shall be deemed fully paid-up, and the rights granted to Investor under Articles 3.00 and 3.01 shall include the right to grant sublicenses to Third Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.03 If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, the license grants set forth in Articles 3.00 and 3.01 will become exclusive to Investor for a term of ten years, shall not be subject to a licensing fee, the granted licenses in favor of the Investor shall be deemed fully paid-up, and the rights granted to Investor under Articles 3.00 and 3.01 shall include the right to grant sublicenses to Third Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term of the exclusive license from 'perpetual' to 'ten years' in Article 3.03 directly contradicts the implication in Article 16.00 that the agreement continues until the expiration of the last patent. While Article 16 is talking about agreement, Article 3.03 talks about license grants, which creates an in-text contradiction. This contradiction introduces uncertainty about the intended duration of the license and its enforceability beyond the ten-year period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16.00 Unless earlier terminated in accordance with the terms of this Article XVI, this IP Agreement and the licenses granted herein will continue in effect from the Effective Date until the expiration of the last to expire of the Patents and any additional period of time thereafter that any of the Patents remain enforceable such as in the United States where a party can sue for infringement after a patent expires and seek damages for any infringement of the patent during the six years immediately preceding the filing of a suit for infringement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the license was 'perpetual', while the changed text limits the exclusive license to 'ten years'. Article 16.00 states the agreement and licenses continue until the expiration of the last patent. The changed term in Article 3.03 directly contradicts Article 16.00 by imposing a ten-year limit on a license that should last until patent expiration.

perturbation.original_text: 16.01 Investor at any time may provide written notice to Company of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Company fails to cure the identified breach within thirty (30) days after the date of the notice, Investor may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.01 Investor at any time may provide written notice to Company of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Company fails to cure the identified breach within sixty (60) days after the date of the notice, Investor may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the cure period for a material breach from thirty (30) days to sixty (60) days in Article 16.01 creates a direct in-text contradiction with the thirty (30) day cure period stated in Article 16.02 for Company's breach if the Option is not exercised. This creates ambiguity and makes enforcement unpredictable, as it's unclear which cure period applies to which party or circumstance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16.02 If the Option is not exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may provide written notice to Investor of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Investor fails to cure the identified breach within thirty (30) days after the date of the notice, Company may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Investor. If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may not terminate this IP Agreement for material breach.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 60 days in Article 16.01. This directly contradicts Article 16.02, which specifies a 30-day cure period for Investor's breach if the Option is not exercised. This inconsistency creates ambiguity and makes enforcement unpredictable, as it is unclear which cure period applies.
